content.text: An agent does not exist in isolation — it interacts with an
: the file system, REST APIs, databases, shell processes, browsers, or other agents. Each environment exposes a different interface, but the agent sees them all through a uniform
abstraction. This separation lets you swap production environments for test doubles, enforce safety boundaries at a single chokepoint, and reason about what the agent can and cannot touch.

attrs.alt: Agent interacting with six environment types — file system, HTTP API, database, shell, browser, and other agents

content.text: directly in tool implementations, define a thin abstract interface that all environment adapters implement. The agent's tools delegate to this interface; the specific adapter behind it can change without touching the agent logic:
— retrieves a resource. The meaning of
depends on the adapter: a file path, a URL path segment, a SQL query, or a shell command.
— creates or updates a resource. On the file system this writes a file; over HTTP it POSTs or PUTs; in a shell adapter this is typically unsupported.
— enumerates available keys under a prefix. Lets the agent discover what is accessible without knowing the exact key.
— a uniform result container so callers never need to catch adapter-specific exceptions in the tool dispatch layer.
The file system adapter sandboxes the agent to a
) are blocked by resolving every key relative to the root and rejecting any path that escapes it:
before comparing to the root. On systems with symlinks, a naive
check on the raw string can be bypassed by a symlink that points outside the root.
follows symlinks, so the resolved path reflects the actual filesystem location.
to POST (or PUT). Authentication headers are injected once at construction time — tool implementations never handle credentials directly:
keeps the adapter flexible without requiring a separate
method. The same pattern works for PATCH and DELETE. Rate-limit handling and retry logic belong in the executor layer, not here.
Shell access is the highest-risk environment type. An allow-list of safe commands prevents the agent from running arbitrary executables, while a subprocess timeout prevents infinite hangs:
— block-lists are incomplete; new dangerous commands are constantly added. Allow only exactly what the agent needs.
— most shell interactions are read-only (list files, grep, count lines). Disabling writes eliminates an entire class of accidents.
— set a short timeout (5–10s) to catch infinite loops or hung subprocesses before they stall the agent loop.

Swapping a real adapter for an in-memory double makes agent tests fast, deterministic, and side-effect-free. The double also acts as a spy — it logs every write call so tests can assert on what the agent persisted:
to the agent's tool dispatcher instead of the real adapter. Pre-populate it with
data to simulate what the agent would find in production. After the agent runs, inspect
Wiring the Environment into the Agent Loop
Tool implementations are thin: they validate arguments, call the environment interface, and return the result as a string. The agent loop calls the tool; the environment handles the side effect:
Test the adapter implementations and the agent's interaction patterns independently:
For integration tests of the full agent loop, inject an
pre-populated with the files or records the agent will read. After the loop completes, assert on
to verify the agent produced the correct outputs without touching the real file system.

External services fail. When an HTTP API or database goes down, an agent without a circuit breaker will hammer the broken backend on every loop step — burning quota, delaying recovery, and filling the observation window with identical error messages. A circuit breaker trips
after a threshold of consecutive failures and rejects requests immediately until a recovery timeout elapses:
— normal operation. Failures increment the counter; successes reset it.
— all requests fail immediately with a clear "circuit open" message. The agent sees this as a tool error and can decide to escalate or wait.
— one probe request is allowed through after the recovery timeout. If it succeeds the circuit closes; if it fails the circuit stays open and the timer resets.
During development and testing you often want to let the agent
without committing any side effects. A
decorator wraps any adapter and blocks all writes while leaving reads intact:

The read-only guard is also useful as a safety check in production: agents operating in an advisory role (recommending changes, previewing diffs) should be wired to a read-only environment so a prompt-injection attack cannot accidentally trigger writes.
Environment interaction is mediated through a uniform

that decouples tool implementations from specific backends. File system adapters sandbox the agent to a root directory; HTTP adapters inject auth once at construction; shell adapters enforce an allow-list; in-memory doubles enable fast, deterministic tests. Placing safety boundaries at the adapter layer — path validation, allow-lists, timeouts — means the agent's reasoning logic stays clean, and the same agent code works against production environments and test doubles alike.
